topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification flashed across my screen, a jarring intrusion into the quiet hum of my afternoon. It was a request. The name attached to it sent a familiar, cold knot tightening in my stomach. Mark. Mark Peterson. The boy who used to orchestrate elaborate cruelties in the third grade. My fingers hovered over the 'Accept' button, my breath catching in my throat. A strange, sharp twist of resentment flared behind my eyes. He hadn't spoken to me in over a decade, and now, this. I scrolled through his profile picture—a slightly too-confident grin—and felt a distinct tightening in my chest, a feeling of something vital slipping away. I hesitated for a long moment, the silence of my room amplifying the drumming in my ears. Finally, with a sigh that felt too heavy for my frame, I tapped the button.

[story 2]

I reread the message twice, the words blurring slightly as I tried to process the audacity of it all. It was him. The architect of my early insecurities was trying to reconnect. A flush crept up my neck, hot and immediate, and I instinctively pulled my hoodie tighter around myself. Looking at the request felt like being forced to confront a shadow I’d spent years trying to outrun. I watched the little heart icon appear, a tiny, bright beacon signaling his intent. Inside, a restless churning began. I started pacing the length of my small apartment, my movements jerky, my jaw tight. It wasn't excitement I felt; it was a profound sense of being overshadowed, a sudden, unwelcome comparison that made my own accomplishments feel suddenly small and inadequate.

[story 3]

When the alert pinged, I froze mid-sip of my lukewarm tea. The name on the screen was a punch to the gut. I could practically feel the phantom sting of his old taunts resurfacing. My hands, resting on the counter, began to tremble almost imperceptibly. I stared at the profile, my gaze lingering on the years that had passed between us, and a bitter taste coated the back of my tongue. A slow, deliberate exhale escaped me, ragged and shallow. I didn't want to accept; the thought of engaging with him felt like inviting an old wound to reopen, just to watch him observe the damage. The air in the room seemed to thicken, heavy with unspoken comparisons to whatever life he was currently leading.
